museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Across Britain, home computers arrive beneath Christmas trees. The Spectrum is booming, the Commodore 64 is thriving, and the Amstrad CPC 464 is rapidly becoming the machine many children will remember as their first real computer.
museum.curatorNote: December 1984 is less about a single blockbuster release and more about a cultural shift. For thousands of British families, this was the Christmas that gaming moved permanently into the home.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: A Christmas defined by home computers rather than arcade cabinets.

items.title: The Amstrad CPC 464 becomes the must-have Christmas computer
items.body: Having launched earlier in the year, Amstrad's all-in-one machine enters its first Christmas season. Its built-in cassette deck and monitor bundles make it especially attractive to families buying their first computer.

items.title: The UK microcomputer war intensifies
items.body: The Spectrum, Commodore 64, BBC Micro and Amstrad CPC are all competing for space in British homes. Software publishers are increasingly releasing games across multiple formats.

items.title: Arcades remain popular but no longer dominate
items.body: For many younger players, the most exciting machine is no longer in the amusement arcade. It is sitting beneath the television at home.

items.title: Budget software reshapes gaming
items.body: Cheap cassette games are becoming a uniquely British phenomenon. Players can increasingly build collections without spending pocket-money fortunes.

items.title: Bedroom programmers become part of the industry
items.body: Small teams and individual developers continue to create games that compete with products from much larger companies, helping define the UK's emerging games industry.

sections.title: On The Shelves
sections.subtitle: The games and software helping define Christmas 1984.

items.title: Elite
items.body: Still astonishing players with its wireframe universe and sense of freedom.

items.title: Jet Set Willy
items.body: One of Britain's most talked-about home-computer games remains a playground favourite.

items.title: Sabre Wulf
items.body: Ultimate Play The Game continues to set standards for presentation and polish.

items.title: Knight Lore
items.body: Its Filmation engine still looks years ahead of much of the competition.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The machines waiting under wrapping paper.

items.title: Amstrad CPC 464
items.body: A colourful newcomer with a built-in cassette deck and a reputation for making home computing feel less intimidating.

items.title: ZX Spectrum
items.body: Still the dominant force in many British bedrooms thanks to its huge software library and affordable price.

items.title: Commodore 64
items.body: The machine many players considered the premium gaming option thanks to its sound and graphics.

items.title: BBC Micro
items.body: The educational machine that also happened to host one of the greatest games ever made: Elite.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: For most players, discovery still happened face to face.

items.title: The magazine rack was the internet
items.body: News travelled through magazines, adverts and playground conversations rather than online networks.

items.title: Software arrived by post
items.body: Mail-order adverts promised exciting games to anyone willing to wait for delivery.

items.title: Type-in programs still mattered
items.body: Many players were still entering pages of BASIC code from magazines in exchange for simple games.

items.title: The school playground spread rumours
items.body: The fastest way to discover a new game was often hearing about it from somebody whose older brother already owned it.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: The Christmas many British gamers never forgot.

items.title: The computer under the tree
items.body: For thousands of children, Christmas morning 1984 meant discovering a computer waiting beneath the decorations. It wasn't just a toy. It felt like a machine from the future.

items.title: The cassette ritual begins
items.body: Loading a game meant connecting cables, pressing play on a cassette deck and hoping nothing went wrong during the next few minutes.

items.title: Every game felt valuable
items.body: A new cassette could occupy an entire holiday. Players learned levels, drew maps and swapped tips because there was no endless stream of replacements.

items.title: Nobody knew where it would lead
items.body: Some children opening computers that Christmas would grow up to become programmers, journalists, artists and game developers. In December 1984, they were simply excited to see what happened when they typed LOAD.

editorNotes: Museum-style editorial pass focused on the UK home-computer Christmas boom and the arrival of the Amstrad CPC 464.
